review_title: 2 Years!! STILL NOT SENT BY AMAZON.co.uk
review_text: In November 2023 I placed & paid for an order which is sold & dispatched by Amazon themselves.I was informed that the order would have free delivery & be delivered 6 months from the order date which was fine by me.Every six months since then I have received an email telling me the order is delayed - this has gone on for two years now!The item (chemical resistant gloves, 10 pairs) was, & still is, being sold on amazon.One year later, in Nov 2024, i ordered the same item(s) again ..and again I've received the same emails to say there is a delay in shipping. So, all in all, I have now been waiting 2 years & 1 year respectively for Amazon to fulfill their contract with me having taken my money & sent nothing in return apart from empty promises.I have been in touch with their customer services who are next to useless & just regurgitate the "we can only see what you see" spiel. When I have gotten through to a "manager" (through my own insistence) I get he usual promises of "we'll get a request out to our other delivery centres to supply the order..blah blah" & then nothing happens bar the usual email 6 months later telling me it's delayed.SO WHY SAY ALL OF THIS? HERE'S WHY..If you, Amazon, do not have the items you are advertising for sale then take them off your site & inform customers that their orders are not possible rather than keeping folk hanging around for literally nothing.I will keep these two orders open just to see if we can break any World record for the slowest delivery time achieved by any company.I have since found much better & far cheaper gloves on EBAY which were delivered promptly & with no fuss or bother. It's obvious to see who the clear winner is here.

Srishti was the only reason I did not cancel my Amazon Prime, Audible, Paramount, and every other subscription or payment I make to Amazon, and there were many reasons to go.My recent experience with Amazon had been so poor that, on principle, I was ready to walk away from every part of it. Within two weeks, I had two third-party sellers, SongMics and VanHaus, fail to deliver their products and or return my money.When I sought a refund, Amazon’s response was, “They’re a third-party seller; you’ll have to speak with them.”Then the sellers responded, “You’ll have to speak with the courier; we’re not responsible for delivery.”That is not how consumer protection works. You cannot absolve yourself of liability by hiding behind intermediaries when those intermediaries are effectively Amazon distributors due to the volume of business they conduct on this platform.Amazon initially ignored the very clear issues I raised. Were it not for Srishti’s professionalism and persistence, I would have taken my business elsewhere for good.Because of her, I remain an Amazon customer. I’m content to do so when Amazon holds to its promise of fast, reliable service and accountability. That is, after all, the reason we pay for Prime, to receive what we order, every time, on time, and with priority.As a customer of over a decade who has spent a substantial amount with Amazon, I expect far better. Instead, I spent hours in frustration, dealing with third parties who simply do not care. They will receive their one-star reviews and formal complaints to the relevant UK agencies responsible for enforcing business standards and consumer law, and deservedly so.In the end, Amazon made it right, thanks entirely to Srishti. Her conduct reflects what Amazon should always be: efficient, responsive, and accountable.Thank you, Srishti. You turned a lost customer into a retained one.

Amazon delivered a package to my front porch. Per order history and status report, it is lightbulbs. The package is about 75% wet all the way through. On first look, I thought something had leaked out of the box. I checked my order history and there is nothing there that  would leak. The Amazon associate confirmed that it was in fact lightbulbs. I don’t know if there’s something in the box that shouldn’t be or if something leaked on the box. Not knowing what the liquid substance is, I asked the customer service associate to have it picked up and credit my account. After much back-and-forth, his attitude getting more and more condescending by the second, he informed me that I could return it for a refund or a replacement, or I could pay them $7.99 to pick it up. He informed me that I would have to touch it. He even suggested that I open it and try the lightbulbs to see if they might still work. He ignored the fact that I was concerned about what the liquid might be. I think it’s time to start doing more local shopping and wean myself off of Amazon. As long as your order is correct and there are no issues, it’s great. If you have an issue of any kind though, good luck because to quote Mr. Condescension, ‘I will not be making an exception’. at some point, he said something about a coupon, but by that point his arrogant tone had pissed me off, and I more or less told him what he could do with himself and his coupon. Not my most shining moment, but I can’t say I feel all that bad about it. Customer service reps don’t need to be condescending to their customers. I will pick it up with a bunch of paper towels, throw it in a bag and throw it in the garbage, and I’ll end up paying for lightbulbs that are in the trash.

I’m a 100 % disabled U.S veteranI’m a 100 % disabled U.S. Army veteran, and I’ve never been treated with such disregard by two corporations that claim to care about their customers.A K&F Concept tripod sold through Amazon.com catastrophically failed under normal indoor use and destroyed more than $5,000 USD of my professional photography gear — including a Canon R5 Mark II, Canon 100 mm Macro L lens, and Godox ring flash.All of that equipment was brand-new, less than two months old.Amazon assigned Sedgwick Claims Management to handle my case.For months I provided every piece of documentation they requested — purchase receipts, repair estimates, photos, and serial numbers.Instead of help, I got silence, delays, and finally a denial with no explanation.Not once did anyone take accountability.I’ve now had to pay over $5,000 out of pocket to repair what a defective product sold on Amazon destroyed.Because of the stress and financial loss, my service-connected PTSD and chronic pain have worsened.I’ve needed extra doctor visits and medication that the VA is now reviewing for reimbursement.That means Amazon’s negligence has affected federal medical costs — not just me personally.Both the Federal Trade Commission and the Texas Department of Insurance have been notified of Amazon and Sedgwick’s bad-faith handling.I’ve cited legal precedents such as Bolger v. Amazon (2020) and State Farm v. Amazon (2019), which confirm Amazon can be held liable for defective third-party products.If this is how veterans and disabled customers are treated, the public needs to know.I have screenshots, call logs, and every email saved proving how poorly this was handled.Amazon and Sedgwick’s actions have cost me clients, income, and peace of mind.No one should have to fight this hard to be treated fairly after a company’s product fails and destroys their livelihood.(“Claim # 4A2509X146C0001”)

review_title: My account got closed for using a grant card, customer service unreachable
review_text: Last week I received a $60 grant card (virtual Visa card) as game jam prize from a non-profit organization. I entered it on Amazon to buy a router that costs the same. However, they had accidentally put only $50 on the card, so my order got rejected a few minutes later. I contacted them, and they immediately added $10 to the card. I ordered again, and this time, the order was successful.Only five minutes later, I got an email from Amazon saying "Sign in to resolve: Your Amazon account is temporarily on hold". Apparently, in the 20 minutes my account has been existing for, I've conducted "suspicious activities". I followed the instructions in the email, uploaded all required documents, and submitted my request.The next day, I got another email saying that my account remains on hold, because "the document provided did not have the last 4 digits of the card used". At this point, I'm convinced that Amazon's customer service is either made up by bots or completely illiterate idiots, because the last 4 digits of the card were clearly visible.So I resubmitted the whole form, although this time it was asking for the bank routing number. One day later, I got another email. Surprise, my account was still suspended. I went through the whole process once again, included emails from the game jam, billing statements, full-page screenshots of the site, and my governmental ID. By then, I had become frustrated, so I ordered my stuff on AliExpress, where it even costs less than on Amazon.Yesterday, a final message from Amazon arrived, saying that my account has been closed PERMANENTLY. Now, when I sign in on Amazon.com, the following message shows:"The information you provided is insufficient to verify your payment ownership. Any pending orders have been canceled. If you believe this is a mistake, please contact Amazon Customer Service."As of course this is a mistake, I went to the customer service page, and there it gets REALLY interesting. Because when I click on "Contact", it requires me to log in! How am I supposed to log in if my account is closed?

review_title: business account process issues & invoicing not legally correct
review_text: I created a business account on Amazon after being a long-time private customer, but my experience has been extremely disappointing.First, Amazon deleted my new business account without any explanation. After contacting support, the account was restored, but further issues quickly followed. Despite multiple emails and reminders, I never received a proper reply.When I finally made my first purchases, I received incorrect invoices from several third-party sellers — invoices missing my company name and/or VAT number. Amazon’s support claimed they are not responsible for these invoices, which is legally incorrect. As the platform provider, Amazon is responsible for ensuring that all invoices issued through its system comply with EU VAT and invoicing regulations.To make matters worse, one seller confirmed that Amazon does not even forward my company’s VAT number to third-party sellers — something I could also verify in my own business profile, where no VAT number is displayed. This makes it impossible to receive valid VAT-compliant invoices.If Amazon expects customers to manually chase third-party sellers for correct invoices, that is completely unacceptable. The invoicing process must work automatically and comply with EU business and tax standards. For this reason, I have decided not to place any further business orders with Amazon.

review_title: Amazon claimed my card declined when…
review_text: Amazon claimed my card declined when they hadn’t attempted to charge it and mislead me about the delivery date.My complaint is for both Amazon’s system as well as their customer service.I believe that the first representative I spoke with was simply working with the information in which he was provided. However, there was a huge mistake in Amazon’s system.Here’s my story:I made an order on Amazon on September 21st and it was “approved”, and I received an immediate email with the receipt and details.On September 28th, I was emailed the expected delivery date.Then I received an email on October 5th that the payment had declined.So, I went onto the website multiple times to try to find a page which allowed me to fix the issue but nothing came up, even when using the link they provided in the email.I figured it was an accidental email.I received another email the following day (October 6th) indicating it still wasn’t functioning and finally found a way to update my payment method, however the information was already correct. I clicked update regardless.I then received another email (October 7th) that the payment was declined, and so I went online again.Again, I found no way from my end to retry the payment.At this time the items still said they were expected by October 7th/8th.I proceeded to call to ask about this problem and the man on this call verified that the payment hadn’t gone through. So I asked him to retry the payment method and he said it wasn’t working.He told me to call my bank so I did (1 minute after hanging up) and I found out that there had been no declined payments from Amazon. However, they said that a payment had gone through (within the hour) for an Amazon order.So, because I had just been told that the payment didn’t work, I called Amazon once again to verify that it had worked on their end.On my second call, I was told it had gone through but was expected by the 12th of October instead of the 7th.Yet the website had told me just 30 minutes before that it was expected on the 7th.So why would Amazon give me an “up-to-date” delivery date when the package hadn’t even left the warehouse because of the “declined” (or never attempted) payment.And - since they had, why wouldn’t the shipping be expedited due to multiple systematic errors?I asked the representative on the second call if she could do anything about the late delivery date, and she said she couldn’t despite Amazon being at fault for the issue in the first place.Anyways with the misleading emails indicating everything had gone through, the inaccurate delivery information indicating the package would come “Today” on the 7th of October (2025), and the failure to charge the card listed on the account despite its accuracy throughout the whole process, I am appalled.- And on top of all that, they couldn’t even offer solutions to the problem on the website or helpful information on the phone call.

review_title: Very Disappointed After 15 Years as a Loyal Customer
review_text: I’ve been an Amazon customer for over 15 years, but this experience has been one of the worst I’ve ever had with them.Several of my orders were suddenly canceled without explanation, and I spent a full week calling customer service every day trying to resolve the issue. During my last call (48 hours before receiving their latest email), the representative clearly told me that if nobody contacted me within 24 hours, Amazon would send all my canceled items for free to make up for the delay and inconvenience.However, today I called again and was told that they can’t do anything and that I have to reorder everything myself. After so many calls, conflicting answers, and broken promises, I honestly expected better from a company I’ve trusted for so many years.This whole experience was frustrating, time-consuming, and disappointing. Amazon’s customer service used to be reliable — now it feels like they no longer care about their loyal customers.Order number: 701-7604966-3682667

I ordered a dress for a Girl child for the age group 3-4 years. The order was delivered, but the dress size was small. Hence I placed an order to replace this dress with one for the age group 4-5 years. I was informed by Amazon to keep the dress delivered earlier ready and that would be collected from me when the replacement dress is delivered.Then on the day when the replacement dress was to be delivered, I get a message from Amazon that it has already been delivered. However, the replacement dress was not delivered, and the previously delivered dress has not been collected by Amazon.If you have noted, Amazon does not provide a phone number for customer service on its App now. Your only option is to write a feedback stating the package was not delivered as claimed. Then the message states that your feedback has been submitted. Be advised, that this is just taken as a feedback from your end, and not a complaint. Again, it is not the first time that this has happened to me where Amazon has claimed to have made the delivery without doing so. Any company that is in such a service industry and does not have a customer service number is not an ethical company. That itself is a tacit acknowledgement by the company that this is a general practice from its end. Even after I have informed the company in the Orders section of their App that the order has not been delivered as claimed by them, there is no call, message or email from their end.Now I leave it to the judgement and understanding of the readers to analyze for themselves.So to summarize, better to look for credible alternatives for Amazon. If that is not possible, I would like to print the name of the Amazon merchant here from whom the dress was purchased. That merchant name is A. R. U. N (All Things Uber Nice). I would not touch them with a pole in future!This is a small price to pay to learn a big lesson!

review_title: Be wary. Amazon is a BIG company, that doesn’t automatically qualify Amazon as a GOOD company to work for:
review_text: I was an employee for almost three years. I was terminated over having to miss an MET day. (mandatory extra time) My schedule was Wednesday-Saturday 1:20am to 11:50am.DIN3 in South Bend Indiana moved to a brand new, beautiful, facility. When we getting ready to move MET was called for at DIN3 in order to get everything done before the move. The new facility in Elkhart is DI115. The weekend before Labor Day Weekend I had plans in place for both my grown children to stay with me. Amazon called for MET barely 25 hours before the mandatory day was set to start (which would be Sunday morning at 1:20am) I frantically noticed my kids that I suddenly had to work and said we needed to reschedule their visit.I made the shift and our time together was rescheduled for the following weekend, which was Labor Day Weekend. So I work my regular week Wed-Sat 10 hour shifts per day and Amazon pulls the same stunt by demanding MET however that notification went out SO LATE I was already on my way in for my Saturday shift. I was made aware of the mandatory extra time day during the first part of my shift by another employee who was very upset; as whoever made the decision to call for an MET day so extraordinarily late and on the Sunday of Labor Day Weekend obviously didn’t take into consideration employees that may have had plans or employees that may have had children.My daughter is fighting cancer and I hadn’t seen my son but one time this summer. AND THIS WAS OVER LABOR DAY WEEKEND. . In my opinion it’s an unfair practice to not allow enough time to change plans and if employees have plans that just can’t be changed not allow enough time to get those shifts covered. Their blanket policy states if you miss too much time an go negative UPT you’re terminated. Period. I notified Amazon that I had missed my shift immediately and even suggested it was an unfair practice and requested hi the 10 UPT hours they took to be reinstated. So after I missed that Sunday (I got to see my kids Labor Day Sunday and go to Church together and following Church take them to brunch) I went back to work the following week and everything was normal. It took two weeks for Amazon to fire me and by that time I had almost earned all the time I missed back. If I had been allowed to work another week (like others have in the past) I wouldn’t have been upside down.Nope-I was terminated. I appealed the termination but unfortunately the hiring manager (who doesn’t not have children)(there’s nothing wrong with that but maybe someone who has children would better understand the needs of a higher percentage of employees-just sayin)said it was straight forward and since I was negative UPT termination is final.**A few things Amazon is missing the boat on. I’m an older employee. I’m very capable and had a very successful 31 year career before joining Amazon. I’m dependable and have a good work ethic-Amazon fails to see there may be much more value to the senior citizen employee vs being at every shift for every hour.My case also falls under the FMLA responsibilities Amazon owes to their employees but whoever did my hearing did not see that as an option. The absolute most disappointing thing about all of this is seeing who they re-hired to fill my spot. My only hope is if my old manager somehow comes across this review and takes it up the ladder.I liked my job and I was good at it. I was an asset for Amazon-blanket policies are still policies

INEXCUSABLE FAILURE: A Company Wasting Our Time With 19th-Century TrackingTo my fellow customers, we must be clear: Amazon's delivery service is fundamentally broken and actively disrespectful of our time.It is astonishing that a company built on logistics and technological advancement consistently fails at the most basic promise: reliable delivery. Widespread dissatisfaction is the norm, not the exception. We are all subjected to the same infuriating cycle of driver instructions being ignored, packages being abandoned in exposed, unsafe locations, and frequent delivery errors that send shipments to the wrong address. The commitment to promised delivery times is a joke; "reliability" simply does not exist in your vocabulary.My recent experience highlights this systemic incompetence perfectly. We were told of a delivery scheduled for Monday. This requires clearing an entire day—a precious, non-refundable 24 hours—to wait. We dutifully kept the whole day open, only to receive a single, vague message in the early afternoon: “The van has left for delivery.”That was the last meaningful update. We wasted the entire day, waiting, watching, and being held hostage by an archaic, inefficient system. As I write this, we are still waiting, our productive time utterly nullified.Stop Treating Our Time as DisposableIn a world where children’s toys have live GPS tracking and simple food apps show the exact location of the courier, it is absolutely unacceptable that a multi-billion dollar logistics giant cannot provide accurate, minute-by-minute tracking.This is not rocket science. With the technological advancements available today, your delivery vans, drivers, or central dispatch could easily utilize a basic smartphone and tracking software to provide:The driver’s current location relative to the route.An accurate Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA).Confirmation that the route is being followed efficiently.The failure to implement this is not a technical oversight; it is a deliberate corporate negligence that maximizes your convenience while deliberately frustrating and disrespecting the customer.We demand better. We demand reliable service and tracking technology that is current, not decades old. Until you fix this core infrastructure failure, you are simply a company that prioritizes profits over the basic respect owed to every paying customer.If you agree, let your voice be heard. We deserve better than wasted days and broken promises.

review_title: Let me briefly describe Amazon to you
review_text: Let me briefly describe Amazon to you.Amazon is a very absurd place that calls itself an online marketplace, yet does everything possible to stop you from spending money there, putting maximum effort into making your experience on their platform as complicated as possible.I recently started buying digital game keys on my personal account. My account has my personal phone number attached, two-factor authentication is enabled, but despite all that, after every second order Amazon cancels all my orders and “kindly” sends me an email saying that third parties may have gained access to my account and I need to go through certain procedures (seven circles of hell). I go through all of that, I write to support, explaining to some clueless Indian guy that it’s me making all these purchases, that I have two-factor authentication, nobody can hack me, so just leave me alone and let me go about my business.On top of that, the keys you buy with your own money don’t even arrive immediately — you have to wait up to 4 hours, supposedly “for your protection.” This went on three times in two days, they completely wrecked my nerves, the same thing over and over again, I felt like I was stuck in a time loop being tortured on repeat. And guess what happened on the third day? I got kicked out of my account. When I tried to log back in — surprise! — they asked me to confirm by email that it was really me. I did that. Then they asked me to change my password. I did that. Then they asked me to enter an OTP code from the authenticator. I did that too. And guess what? After I successfully passed every single verification step, they just went ahead and “temporarily” blocked me xD.Honestly, it’s hard to imagine a more monstrously idiotic system. Whoever came up with this deserves a medal, because they’ve literally invented reverse marketing: putting in maximum effort not to sell, but to avoid selling and drive customers away.And if you think that was the peak of idiocy — not so fast! The cherry on top is the fact that after the temporary block, I got a message on the screen: “To resolve this issue, contact customer support.” But when you click to contact them, it asks you to log into the account that has just been blocked ahahahahahahahaha.I decided to call their number and asked the operator to call me back since I don’t live in the US and international calls are very expensive. Instead of calling back, she sent me an email with instructions saying I need to log in to my account in order to contact support (genius thoughts chase them endlessly, but they’re always one step ahead). Then I tried calling again. Another one of our beloved Indian guys picked up, promised he’d call me back, but after I hung up he just sent me another email saying they can’t call outside the US. So basically, me — just an ordinary user — can afford to make an international call, but a multibillion-dollar company cannot? Makes sense, right?I don’t know, it seems like Amazon has lost its mind. They are putting in so much effort just to drive their own customers away, essentially doing their competitors a huge favor by sending them a steady flow of disappointed users.

review_title: Deeply Disappointed by Amazon’s Bereavement Support
review_text: Following the sudden passing of my grandfather, my family and I have been trying to close his Amazon account. This should have been a simple request during an already difficult time. Unfortunately, the experience has been deeply frustrating and distressing.Despite clearly explaining multiple times that my grandfather passed away unexpectedly and did not leave written authorisation or photo ID (which he never had), our explanations have been repeatedly ignored. Instead of proper guidance, we received irrelevant and automated responses, including one email warning me about closing my own account. This was completely unrelated to the issue at hand.Attempts to clarify via live chat were equally disappointing. I was met with copy-pasted responses that showed no sign of having read my messages or understanding the situation.This lack of basic empathy and support from such a large company is appalling. The process has placed avoidable stress on my family while we are already coping with loss. At the very least, I expected clear, human communication and a willingness to help in a bereavement case. Instead, we have been met with silence, delays, and robotic responses.If Amazon has a bereavement team, they need serious training, both in compassion and in simply reading what is being sent to them. A truly disappointing experience.

As of September 24, 2025, I have deliberately removed all of my positive reviews from Trustpilot. This was not because those businesses failed me, but because Trustpilot itself has proven corrupt.They permanently censored my legitimate, fully documented review of eBay (submitted as a registered LLC seller with proof of business activity). Even after providing evidence, their “Content Integrity Team” declared the case closed, keeping my review offline while allowing companies like Rakuten to challenge or suppress critical feedback.By leaving only my negative reviews here, I want to make clear: the imbalance is created by Trustpilot, not by me. My contributions were balanced across 1–5 stars until Trustpilot chose to silence the critical side.This platform does not operate in good faith. I will no longer post reviews here. Instead, I will be reporting my findings to regulators and leaving reviews on platforms Trustpilot cannot manipulate.On September 16, 2025, I attempted to purchase a Lenovo IdeaPad laptop (Order #111-5903766-7040259) using the 6-month equal pay financing option Amazon displayed at checkout with my Amazon Prime Secured Card. Instead of processing the first installment, the order failed with “Payment revision needed.”Misrepresentation of Financing EligibilityAmazon’s checkout explicitly showed the financing option on my secured card.Synchrony later told me the denial was tied to my card’s credit limit. But that makes no sense — the purpose of installments is to allow purchases above the balance by spreading payments over time. If the plan requires the full limit to be available upfront, then it is not financing at all.Synchrony did not say all secured cards are excluded. Some cardholders do get approved, others don’t. That inconsistency — combined with Amazon still displaying the option — makes the system misleading.Declines Even Under the LimitMy card had $175 available on a $200 limit.I attempted smaller transactions, including a $150 Amazon e-gift card. All failed with the same “Revise payment” error. These were well under the limit. Had they succeeded, I could have split the laptop purchase, but Amazon’s system blocked even that.Customer Service Harm and LockoutAfter multiple transfers in chat, I was connected with rep Jasper Shezhi. Following his instructions and/or direct actions, my account was immediately placed on hold.Since then, I’ve been stuck in a permanent OTP loop: I receive codes by phone and email, but they never unlock my account. This lockout began only after his involvement and has now lasted for approximately one week.Loss of Paid ServicesBecause of the lockout, I cannot log in to Amazon.I have lost access to Prime, my Fire Stick, and all subscriptions tied to it.Accountability FailureAmazon blames Synchrony. Synchrony blames Amazon.Neither has taken responsibility for:Showing financing that could not actually be used,Blocking valid smaller purchases, andAn agent’s actions that left my account locked.Orders involved:#111-5903766-7040259#111-9909208-8612238#111-1574513-0826657Note there may be other order numbers involved, but due to the lockout, I can no longer check.Until Amazon and Synchrony correct these issues, I cannot recommend either. Their systems are misleading, their policies inconsistent, and their customer service has left me worse off than before I asked for help.

review_title: I must remember to shop elsewhere and never use Amazon again!
review_text: Utterly abhorrent experiences. This last time I spent the whole of Friday evening (12 September) asking questions about a specific product (Botslab G980H 3K 4Channel Dashcam) The Amazon page listed very few items that made up the dashcam, so I asked if it came with wiring. The agent simply ‘pasted and copied’ their list, no wiring shown. I pressed and was told all I would receive was what was listed. This was several hours into ‘Chatting with an associate,’ so I left and contacted Botslab live chat in USA (Fortunately due to the time difference the chat was ‘live’) I was assured the box included wiring and was sent a picture of ‘What’s in the box?’ This showed several items Amazon did not list. I got back to Amazon and told them they were wrong, the different coloured ‘associates’ I had been, and was in contact with didn’t care, it was a case of ‘Take your chance.’ So, after what seemed a lifetime on that Friday evening, but was only around 4 -5 hours, I ordered the dashcam as well as the hardwire kit.Both items showed discounts (5% and 10%) that ‘will be added at checkout’ as I proceeded but, having gone through the motions and placing the Order, no discount code was added, and it stated that the discount code for the hardwire kit could not be applied. That led to even more online chats with ‘associates.’ Two different ‘associates’ assured me that if I ordered the items and contacted Amazon once I’d received them, they would refund 10% to cover both items, one ‘associate’ even sent me an email to confirm what I was being told. The dashcam cost £169.99 and the hardwire kit cost £19.99 so I expected a refund of around £19. When I first viewed these items, they showed delivery would be on Monday (15 September) but, having spent a lifetime chatting with ‘associates’ delivery had moved to Tuesday, but during the process I was offered a ‘Free 30-day Prime Trial’ which I signed up to, as this way I would have my items the next day.Now, unlike every other Courier, Amazon gives a six-hour delivery slot instead of the usual two-hour, so you have to waste a day waiting for them to deliver. Okay, having taken delivery near the end of the timeslot, I checked out the goods. All was well, and I was pleased with my purchase, having read up on the item and watched plenty of videos about how they worked and the installation. Then came time to contact Amazon to say I’d received my goods and to claim my refund.In only fifteen minutes of ‘chatting to an associate’ I was passed between five, YES FIVE! different coloured ‘associates.’ Eventually, despite sending the text of the email I had received, I was told by the last two that I had been given ‘misinformation,’ and Amazon could not issue a refund AFTER purchase. I was more than unhappy and asked how I could contact Amazon Corporate, the guy told me he would email me details (I’m still waiting)I phoned Amazon (0800 028 8205) and spoke to a lovely, helpful Irish lady, who listened and then spoke to a manager, came back to me and put me through to that Manager. Unfortunately, once again a different coloured lady, more than difficult to understand, who told me she had taken all the details and would pass them on up the line, in time I would be contacted via email. All I have received is two emails telling me that Amazon do not do refunds. Clearly, they are nothing but thieves who will do whatever is necessary to take YOUR money from YOU!

review_title: Appalling and unprofessional service from Amazon
review_text: I reside in an apartment block with security entry; there are 28 apartments and most residents help each other, especially with acceptance of deliveries; many of us allow couriers entry with deliveries for other residents (in case they are away from the apartment or indisposed) and check that they are securely inside the secure foyer. Couriers usually ring the apartment bell and we answer, allowing access for the parcel to be either delivered to the apartment or at least placed in the secure foyer entrance, where they can be collected later.On this occasion, I had ordered from Amazon two 256GB Memory Cards, valued just under £150, and they were due for delivery today, 7 September. I was at home, in the apartment, all day and received a message from Amazon that the memory cards had been left in my postbox. This is not unusual with Amazon, when the parcel is small and fits into the external, secure postbox.I made my way to the foyer entrance and went to check the letterbox but, outside in the porch, adjacent to the footpath that is open to the public, were FOUR PARCELS, one for me and the other three for other residents inside the apartment block. There was no sign of a delivery vehicle or a parcel. They had been left outside the secure entrance foyer in full view of passing public; it is adjacent to the public footpath and open to any opportunist thief that may pass and we have a significant amount of foot traffic throughout the day and on most days. I immediately retrieved all four parcels, ensured they were allsecure and informed my neighbours of the circumstances of the incident and where the parcels had been retained.My wife and I had been at home in our apartment all day. No attempt had been made to alert either of usof the presence of the courier or the parcel; the entrance button for the apartment had not rung. There was no sign of the courier.This is appalling and irresponsible service. There was not even a pretence of security or concern for us as customers, just another dump and run from an unprofessional courier! The courier has not shown respect to me, the customer, nor for the security of my parcel. How can I be expected to trust Amazon in the future with any parcel that I might order?When I attempted to write a review to Amazon to help remedy such appalling service in the future, I followed the rigmarole until it got to the stage where, because I couldn’t ’tick-a-box’ to finish the review/feedback, there is no way to report such gross failure.

I was expecting a delivery from Amazon between the hrs of 4 am and 8 am today, Sept 4, 2025.I watched on the Amazon app, following the location of the driver and reading the updates as to when my delivery would arrive. I live in a gated community, and all deliveries would have to go through a security check. When the driver arrived at the security gatehouse, I went to the front lobby of my building and waited for the driver to arrive. I am looking out the front door in the lobby, and I noticed the delivery driver make a U-turn and exit the area. I called Amazon customer service to let them know that on the Amazon app, it showed that the delivery was attempted. The driver did NOT attempt. This is the third time that this has happened, and I informed Customer Service of this. The lady from Amazon said that she would contact the driver and have him come back. Within 10 minutes, a driver did show up as I was waiting outside the front doors. I commented to the driver as to why he turned around and left before he delivered my packages. He replied that he did attempt and that he did not receive any answer. I pointed out to him the delivery instructions on the Amazon site and the video camera pointing to the outside of the front of the building, which records All interactions for the front of the entrance. He lied to my face, stating that he did attempt. This is the third time that this has happened to me. I am a truck driver myself, and we follow a strict protocol when making deliveries.Maintain contact with the customer through various channels. Phone the customer en route, upon arrival, use building communication to let the customer know that their delivery has arrived.Amazon has on its customer profile page instructions on how the customer is to be contacted during a delivery.Mine is clearly stated on how to contact me when a delivery is to be made using the building's residential electronic panel located inside the front entrance. This driver did not show up in front of my building, did not get out of his car, and did not stop at my address. Furthermore, the driver did not use the building's com panel to contact me.

review_title: Bereavement case handled with shocking lack of care
review_text: I contacted Amazon regarding a bereavement enquiry after my mother passed away. I was not asking for access to her account — only clarification of a single transaction to reconcile her estate.The call handler (Marijana K.) repeatedly sighed, read the same scripted line, and refused escalation despite my request. I had already provided ID and a death certificate, yet was told nothing could be shared without full probate or administration. This was disproportionate under UK GDPR and showed no compassion for the situation.When I insisted on escalation, she refused multiple times. Only after I stated I was recording the call did she agree to “log a complaint” — but no complaint reference was provided. Instead, I received a generic “how did we do” survey email.Since then, I have written multiple times asking for my complaint to be formally logged. Each time, the same agent has ignored this and simply resent the same scripted template about probate. In other words, she is both the subject of my complaint and the person blocking it from being escalated.This is not just poor service; it is negligence in a bereavement case. Families should not be treated like this when dealing with the estate of a loved one.I have now escalated to Executive Customer Relations myself and will also be raising this with the ICO given the GDPR implications.

review_title: Horrible Amazon Policies & Lack of Concern
review_text: On July 25th, I received an item that was completely missing its contents; the bag was empty.  I reported this in the Amazon return system as Missing Parts.  They sent me a replacement (which had the contents inside the bag), and I returned the empty bag that I had received, through the return process (Kohl's).  All of this took place within 2 days.On August 31st, I received an email that I would be charged for the original item that I ordered if I didn't send it back by September 9th.  I sent the package I received back on July 26th, so I should not be charged.  I called Amazon Customer Service (which was extremely difficult to find a phone number to within the app), and was told the following by the first representative I spoke with:1. Since I was calling after the 30 day window, there is nothing they could do to resolve my issue.  It did not matter that I received the email to inform me I would be charged on August 31st (5 days after the 30-day window ended).2.  She shared with me that I selected "defective item" in my return claim, which was not reflective of items being missing.  While I did not think I chose that option, I shared that if I did choose that option, it had to be because "Missing item" was not available.3.  I asked to speak to her manager and she shared that the manager would tell me the same thing, but she transferred me.Below outlines my conversation with the manager; unfortunately, she was short with me and cut me off a few times during our conversation.1. She reiterated that since I was calling after the 30 day window, there is nothing they could do to resolve my issue.  It did not matter that I received the email to inform me I would be charged on August 31st (5 days after the 30-day window ended).  She said the return center that receives the returns sends out the notifications as they process them.  I asked how it could be that I (the customer) is penalized because the return center is behind on their work.  They emailed me on August 31st, after the 30 day window.  So, there was no chance for me to resolve the issue within the policy-outlined time frame.  That did not matter to the manager.2.  Contrary to what the representative shared with me, the manager told me that I selected "missing parts" in my return claim.  I agree that I selected that option and from that it prompted the choice to get a replacement and return what I had originally received.  Therefore, I don't understand why now I'm being charged.  The manager then says that I didn't have missing parts, I had a missing item.  I became upset because at that point she was "playing" with words.  She suggested that since the entire item was missing, I should have called customer service to get it taken care of vs. initiating the return in the app.  I told her you've got to be kidding me.  Missing parts/missing items...to the customer, those are the same things.  And to suggest that I was wrong by not calling in this situation, just to support the company's 30-day policy was crazy to me.3. I understand policy, but there should be ways to make exceptions when the company has dropped the ball.

review_title: Jeff Bezos and Andy Jassy: How Amazon’s Leadership Abandoned American Families and Business Owners  Amazon began as an American success story. Jeff Bezos founded the company in 1994, and over the deca
review_text: Jeff Bezos and Andy Jassy: How Amazon’s Leadership Abandoned American Families and Business OwnersAmazon began as an American success story. Jeff Bezos founded the company in 1994, and over the decades it transformed from an online bookstore into a global marketplace worth trillions. Today, nearly every American family has purchased from Amazon, and millions of small businesses rely on it to reach customers. But behind the slick Prime commercials and polished leadership speeches lies a different truth: Amazon’s leadership—first Bezos, now Andy Jassy—has consistently turned its back on the very Americans who built its empire.The Dark Side of “Customer Obsession”Amazon likes to claim it is “obsessed with customers.” The reality? Thousands of accounts are suspended or banned with little explanation. Sellers wake up to find their livelihoods destroyed. Families suddenly cannot log in to buy diapers, groceries, or household essentials. When they try to get help, they face a maze of offshore call centers that rarely provide real answers.Andy Jassy, who succeeded Bezos as CEO in 2021, has not corrected this problem. Instead, he has allowed account closures and bans to escalate, often with no appeal process that feels fair. For many Americans, Amazon is not a partner in business or family life—it’s an unpredictable giant with unchecked power.Outsourcing: Profits Over American JobsPerhaps the clearest betrayal comes from Amazon’s outsourcing strategy. Despite earning billions from U.S. shoppers and businesses, Amazon has exported most of its customer support to India and other countries. The “Pay by Invoice” department, which directly affects small businesses who buy goods in bulk, is almost entirely handled overseas.This decision hurts Americans in two ways:1. Lost Jobs at Home – After building its fortune on U.S. soil, Amazon chose not to reinvest in American workers. Thousands of well-paying support roles that could have lifted communities were shipped abroad.2. Frustrated Small Businesses – U.S. business owners often deal with communication barriers, time zone delays, and inconsistent support quality. Instead of empowering entrepreneurs, Amazon leaves them battling offshore bureaucracy.Jeff Bezos and Andy Jassy defend these cost-cutting measures as efficiency. But efficiency for Amazon means frustration for American families and fewer jobs for American workers.Bezos’ Image vs. Jassy’s Reality

review_title: I’ve completely given up on searching…
review_text: I’ve completely given up on searching for things on Amazon. The company is incentivized to keep you in a perpetual loop of searching. Every time you perform a search, the initial results are ads, and Amazon earns revenue from these ads, even if the products are completely unrelated to your search query. This practice makes Amazon extremely pleased, as they profit from your exposure to the ads without you being able to find what you’re looking for. It likely works even better if you don’t make any purchases at all, as Amazon doesn’t incur shipping costs for the products, and you’re likely paying a subscription to Amazon.In essence, you’re essentially paying for a service that doesn’t actually fulfill any of your needs.

⭐ 1 Star Review – Amazon’s Disgraceful Automated SystemI have been a loyal Amazon customer for over 20 years, spending thousands of pounds on their platform. Recently, I decided to sell items through Amazon Seller Central — and the experience was absolutely appalling.After providing all the requested documents, Amazon rejected my application with a cold, automated message: “We are unable to verify the documents you provided… you may not sell on Amazon.” No explanation. No conversation. No human review. No chance to appeal. Just a generic dismissal.This shows exactly what Amazon has become: a company that treats people like numbers, making automated decisions that affect people’s lives and livelihoods, with no accountability. A business built on the loyalty of millions of human customers is now run by machines and faceless processes that offer no fairness, no dignity, and no respect.Other platforms — such as eBay — treat sellers with more transparency and flexibility. They give real people the opportunity to trade, rather than picking and choosing who they “allow” to participate. Amazon, on the other hand, hides behind automated systems and canned responses.Amazon’s biggest downfall will be this arrogance. Customers and sellers alike are tired of being treated this way. Many, like myself, are already cancelling services such as Audible, Prime, and Amazon Music, and taking our business elsewhere. We survived without Amazon before, and we can — and will — again.Amazon is no longer a company of people serving people. It is a machine that forgets who built it in the first place: human beings. Until Amazon learns to respect its customers and sellers, it deserves every bit of criticism it receives.Shame on you, Amazon.

review_title: Could  not even buy a pair of socks
review_text: I am fed up with Amazon and will never ever deal with Amazon ever again. I can't even shop on their website anyway. It all started a week ago. I tried to go into my old account and change the password. Well, since I haven't used it in years, it asked me for my zip code. I don't remember which zip code I used years ago and tried many, but none worked. I called Amazon, and they said to open a new account and add your current debit card instead, that the account I am trying to use cannot be accessed anymore, which is totally ridiculous to begin with. So I proceeded to open a new account, added what I wanted to purchase to the cart, and added my Chase debit card. So far so good. I placed the order, my bank approved it, and bam, seconds later the order was canceled due to suspicious payment activity. WHAT? . Ok, I try again. Same result: order canceled. So I called Amazon back and said, "Why are my orders being canceled?" They said they cannot tell me what suspicion they have, but it seems I am a scammer. I have to prove that I am the owner of the card and submit a banking statement with the same name and card number on transactions to prove that the owner of the card is making the transaction, even though CHASE BANK had no qualms it was me. I even called Chase, and they said, "We do not know what the problem is with Amazon." So I proceeded to upload a banking statement exactly as in the video and instruction link they sent me to upload my statement, which had 12 transactions that had the last digits of the same bank card I was using, the same name, the same address, etc. Then get this: I receive an email saying that they have determined that the institution I sent a bank statement from is not (I repeat, not) a US institution. Yes, Chase Bank, the largest US bank, is not a US institution, even though on the statement it has their address and all the customer service numbers on the top right-hand corner. I was like, "Is this a joke?" Now I think I know why Jeff Bezos is now selling his stock in Amazon; it is a completely retarded institution that has been hijacked by retarded Indians in India. So again I called Amazon many times after to get the same result over and over; they tell me to reupload the banking statement many times, and I do, until I finally today said, "At this point I found a better price on the product I wanted, and if Amazon does not approve my account, the hell with Amazon, I don't care." Well, guess what? The last straw today, Here is the email they sent saying they cannot prove that Chase Bank is a legitimate USA institution.Hello,Thank you for taking the time to submit your documents. We understand this process can be frustrating, and we appreciate your cooperation in helping us to maintain your account security.After a careful review, we were unable to verify your identity because the document provided is not a valid state- or country-issued ID proof or a legible utility bill.As a result, your account remains on temporary hold.To restore access, sign in to your Amazon account and follow the on-screen instructions.Once you've provided the required information, we will review it and respond within 24 hours.We've closed your Amazon account because we weren't able to confirm the identity associated with the payment method you provided.We may not reply to further emails about this issue.Yes, they closed my account because they cannot verify that CHASE BANK is a legitimate USA banking institution, and so they are basically saying that MY Chase Bank debit card is not a legitimate form of payment in the United States of America.I would advise anyone reading this not to deal with Amazon; it is nothing but a headache now. In fact, now the seller of the product I was going to buy raised the price by 85 dollars. Since I have been at this whole ordeal with Amazon for 5 days, I don't care anymore. I can buy it for 5 dollars less on eBay and other websites and not deal with the hassle. Without making a single call, I can just buy and wait for it to arrive. So at this point, Amazon can either approve the account or not, or I will never deal with Amazon again.

review_title: Amazon unlawfully sends wrong invoices to the UPS partners abroad causing problems with the customs
review_text: Amazon.com sends wrong commercial invoices to the UPS delivery partners in my country.It's against the law to make such false documents and this can misguide our custom authorities into thinking that I ordered something more expensive than it really is.This can result in me overpaying import duties as they are calculated as a percentage of the price of the item + the shipping.For items with a total value under €22 - no fee is charged, above €22 -I must pay tax and above €90 - tax + customs duties. I'm fine with paying that, but it must be based on realistic invoices.Amazon by law must provide an accurate and itemized invoice, especially when import fees are involved.Note that the online invoice in my Amazon account looks fine, but the problem is the invoice sent by Amazon to the UPS partner. It looks totally different and I receive it by email as a PDF from the local UPS partner.That invoice combines together the price of the item AND the import charges deposit.The invoice for my order #111-1738409-1444259 should read: $56.95 (for the item) + $16.15 (shipping) = $73.10, but the wrong invoice reads: $103 (for the item) + $16.15 (shipping) = $119.15.Here Amazon added the $46.04 deposit to the price of the item for no sane reason, so it looks as if the item costs $103, instead of $56.95.Now I'm afraid that the custom authorities will take more money from my deposit than they should.And since the total value in this case is above €90, maybe I will be charged taxes + custom duties. My original plan was that the value will be below €90.Now I must submit some papers to the custom authorities, but I must copy in those papers the same info written in the invoice. I can't write there what I want.I explained this to the local UPS partner and I sent them screenshots from my Amazon account - but they don't care. For them, the invoice that they received from Amazon is an official document and it's all that matters.I tried to explain all this via Amazon's chat, but Amazon doesn't care. I found some Amazon email adresses, but one of the messages bounced back and they answered the other one, but it seems that they don't understand the problem.It's very simple: make proper invoices.I also contacted the seller, who kindly told me that he's sorry and he explained me that this is not his fault as Amazon sent the parcel.The invoice sent by Amazon to the UPS partner for my order # 113-1907217-8572228 was wrong, too.This is not whining of a spoiled customer, this is about following laws and regulations. This is not a game.

review_title: Disabled customer can't get delivery instructions followed
review_text: I am permanently disabled and live up two flights of stairs. I cannot carry heavy items and sometimes am in too much pain to use the stairs. I'm also recovering from shoulder surgery to my dominant side. Despite that there are very clear delivery instructions on my account that Amazon's own supervisors have written, countless times I have found my packages thrown inside the building on the first floor or even delivered to entirely different buildings. In the last two weeks, I have had to call Amazon twice about this issue. I was, of course, assured it wouldn't happen again. I have made countless calls about this issue, yet it continues. This is a violation of the Americans with Disabilities Act and not a trivial matter.The last time it happened, the delivery driver threw my package on the steps outside the building. This isn't acceptable delivery for any customer, but especially not one who is disabled and has delivery instructions clearly delineated on their account. When I called Amazon about it this time, I spoke with an agent, then a supervisor, and then a supervisor in Logistics. I have done this many times before as well. One of the supervisors I spoke with this last time actually said that many of the drivers really don't care. What a weird statement to make to a customer. I suppose she was trying to distance Amazon the company from what I assume are contractors who make deliveries, but Amazon is responsible for getting the package to me, so there is no excuse. It's ultimately their problem, and they need to own it and resolve it. The supervisors assured me that this misdelivery of my packages won't happen again. I'm naturally skeptical after being reassured umpteen times over the last five years.This incident was the final straw for me after countless misdelivered packages over a span of five years. I filed a complaint about Amazon on the ADA.gov website. I hope they take action against Amazon for not delivering packages appropriately for disabled customers. As one of the supervisors said, EVERYONE should receive their package on their doorstep even if they're not disabled. A package delivered to a customer in an apartment building should be at their door or where they have indicated it should be delivered, not dumped elsewhere, even when there are stairs.

review_title: Disappointed by Repeated Faulty Items & Slow Refunds – Where’s the Customer-Centric Approach?
review_text: As a long-time Amazon customer, I feel increasingly frustrated with the experience of receiving faulty items on multiple occasions. While I understand that returns are a part of online shopping, it’s becoming too frequent and inconvenient.What’s more concerning is the delay in refunds. Being out of pocket for up to ten days while Amazon processes a return adds unnecessary stress—especially when the issue was with the item sent, not my decision to return it. Multiply that by thousands (if not millions) of customers globally, and that’s a significant amount of customer money Amazon is temporarily holding onto.Which leads to the question: is Amazon earning interest on all these pending refunds? It might be technically legal, but is it ethical—especially when customers are left waiting and bearing the inconvenience?It’s hard not to feel disillusioned when you read headlines about Amazon executives hiring out parts of Venice for lavish weddings, while the average customer waits over a week to be refunded for a product that shouldn’t have been faulty in the first place.If Amazon truly prioritises customer satisfaction, why not implement a compensation scheme for delays in refunds or faulty item deliveries? Even small gestures would show customers they're valued beyond just their wallets.Time for Amazon to reflect on whether their actions really align with their customer-first mission—or if that slogan is simply part of the marketing.

The model of Amazon that existed in my mind for the longest time was "sort of shady company--but the customer service is great." Maybe I was lured in by their own branding as "Earth's most customer-centric company"; maybe it was my fault for being gullible. But I was astonished at how bad the customer service was when I recently needed it for the first time.I ordered a soft-plastic laundry backet, a little over two feet tall, and the seller made the crazy decision to crumple it up like a piece of paper and ship it in a little shipping bag. It arrived deformed and wouldn't even stand up right, but I couldn't return it because the bag got destroyed trying to get this huge thing out of it.The first two Amazon reps I worked with just refused to understand my problem. I kept telling them that I couldn't return without packaging, but they--intentionally or unintentionally--misunderstood me. One of them said that I could take it to any post office and it would be packaged for me; one said that the seller would send me new packaging. I don't think I was dealing with AI--but these folks definitely had that AI-flavor of making up whatever they want.The third rep was the worst. He told me that he had authorized a refund for me without doing it. I waited a week expecting a refund before I realized I had been had.The fourth rep helped me open a claim with Amazon A-to-Z which, after everything, was denied. There's no explanation as to why when you're denied. I got sent a damaged item and, due entirely to the seller's packing choices, I didn't have a way to send it back for a return. But: denied. If the A-to-Z policy doesn't cover that, what does it cover?It's twenty dollars, I'll survive. But I'm so embarrassed that I believed the branding about Amazon's customer service. You're a nobody to them. What did you expect from a company like this?

This is challenging. Historically I have received good products, fair pricing, almost always on-time deliveries for my Prime membership... that is until I sold my house, downsized, and moved into a high-rise apartment building. This building has a designated, secure, locked area for all package deliveries. The driver logs the package into the secure area and the resident is notified and provided a code with which to retrieve their package. For official Amazon drivers, they can access the building access code and follow the procedure. HOWEVER, Amazon contracts A LOT of their deliveries to independent drivers, who either do not have access to this information with the security codes for the building or they are not properly trained before being turned loose with a customer's package for delivery. Therefore, the package is either left in an unsecure location or the delivery is not made and the package is delayed until the next day; it has taken 2-3 days before I actually receive my packages when this happens. If the leasing office is open, these contract drivers enter through the main entrance to the building and will often leave the package in front of the resident's door. However, I had an early morning delivery that was supposed to happen today. I needed the product today as early as possible. However, the driver shows up, can't get in the building, calls me, but the only words of English she speaks is: Amazon access? So, I try to tell her how to get in, but she can't understand me. A while later, I get a picture of the package left on top of the bank of mailboxes in the general mailroom. Apparently someone let her into the building. This s the equivalent of a house delivery where the driver pulls onto your street and throws the package on the sidewalk, enters "delivered" into the system and drives off. The customer may never find or receive the actual package because anybody walking by can just pick it up and walk off with it... which is what happened to me today... and it's not the first time this has happened.I order A LOT of stuff through Amazon. I have only lived here a few months, but this contract driver issue is a HUGE PROBLEM when it comes to apartment buildings with designated, secure package areas. I have spoken with Amazon customer service reps. I have spoken with Amazon "supervisors" and yet it still happens when packages are sent to my building or other surrounding buildings by a contract driver. I have spoken with the leasing office at my building and they assure me they have had numerous conversations with Amazon about this problem and NOTHING EVER CHANGES. GET IT TOGETHER, AMAZON! Either stop using contract drivers altogether for secure apartment complexes or train them properly to use the information IN YOUR SYSTEM for the building so they can get our stuff delivered! This is outrageous! UPS has no problem, FedEX has no problem, USPS has no problem when it comes to packages... Official Amazon drivers have no problem... it's just these contract drivers.

review_title: ⭐️⭐️⭐️ Still Hoping Amazon Will Step Up – The Only Company That Actually Listened
review_text: Among all the companies involved in my recent experience — Amazon, Evri, Punch Technology, ProtectYourBubble, and Tesco Insurance — Amazon is the only one where I felt a real human actually listened to me and took the time to respond with empathy and professionalism.That’s why I’m giving three stars for now — not because my issue is resolved, but because Amazon has shown a willingness to help, even if limited by platform rules or third-party sellers.Despite not being directly responsible, Amazon still offered help and tried to guide me — something none of the other companies genuinely did. The others were quick to point fingers, hide behind technicalities, or quietly disappear.🟡 So here’s my promise: I’m leaving this review with three stars, and I will update it to five stars if Amazon takes the lead and finds a way to help resolve this situation.As for the others, their reviews will remain public as they are — because I believe people should know what really happens when things go wrong.⸻🔹 Customer Update – My Full Experience (Please Read Before You Trust This Warranty) 🔹As a customer, here are the facts:1.	I purchased a graphics card with a 3-year manufacturer warranty on Amazon.2.	I also purchased an additional 3-year extended warranty from ProtectYourBubble (via Amazon).3.	I have comprehensive home insurance that covers theft and damage of up to £80,000.Then:•	The product developed a fault.•	The seller (Punch Technology) asked me to return it under warranty. I specifically asked for guidance on the best return method, but instead of providing a DPD return label as advertised on their own website, they simply gave me an address and RMA ticket number.•	I used Evri, the courier most accessible to me.•	Evri’s driver stole the parcel. Evri accepted liability and offered me a £25 refund.•	Amazon said, “Talk to the seller – we’re not responsible.”•	Punch Technology said, “You didn’t ask clearly enough for our return option – we won’t take responsibility.”•	ProtectYourBubble said, “Our insurance only starts AFTER the manufacturer warranty – and by the way, theft isn’t covered anyway.”•	Tesco Insurance said, “Since the item wasn’t stolen from your home, you’re not covered.”So here I am – the customer who bought all the right warranties, followed all the right procedures, and is now being accused of insurance fraud for simply trying to understand who is actually responsible.Let this be a warning:•	When things go wrong, these big names will quietly step away, find technicalities to avoid liability, and even insult you if you insist on your rights.•	Behind these brand names are disconnected departments, outsourced support, and zero accountability.All I ask is this:One of you – Amazon, Evri, Punch Technology, ProtectYourBubble, or Tesco – take responsibility and stop passing the buck.Whether you help or not, this update will appear under every relevant review I’ve left, so others can learn from my experience.

review_title: Never Received My Phone, No Real Help from Amazon
review_text: I ordered the Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra Dual SIM (Titanium Violet) from Amazon in good faith. Unfortunately, the item never arrived — and months later, my issue still hasn’t been resolved.When the package arrived via UPS, it did not contain the phone I had purchased. Instead, there was a note inside the parcel box from Amazon stating that the item would be delivered later. I didn’t panic — I contacted Amazon Customer Service immediately. Several agents advised me to file a police report, which I did in Namibia. The report was validated and followed up by local authorities.Despite this, I was continuously told to wait for a response from the OFM (Order Fulfillment Management) team. When they finally reached out on January 22, they acknowledged issues with my order but offered no real resolution — just a link to refund policy pages and a generic message that I could reply if the email was sent in error. I replied. Still no solution.To make things worse, the courier company confirmed that the item was never delivered, and yet Amazon’s system falsely marked it as delivered. At one point, it was even suggested that I had declined the package — which is simply not true.I’ve received repeated messages saying that due to the age of the order, nothing more can be done — even though the delays were caused by Amazon's own process. I followed every instruction, submitted every document, and waited patiently. But instead of support, I’ve been led through a frustrating loop of empty responses.This was my first major order on Amazon, and the experience has completely shattered my trust in the platform. I’ve been left without the phone and without a refund — a costly and deeply disappointing experience.
Not good as before. More better choice now.

I’ve had Amazon Prime for the past 6 months, and for the first few months, the deliveries were good, always on time, friendly drivers, and a great service. However, over the past 2 months, I have noticed a very sharp decline in their delivery service in my area.First off I had a very bad experience a few weeks ago when a large parcel got lost twice and ended up arriving 4 days late. That was through DPD delivering for Amazon.The second and frankly last straw, for a very bad delivery, was today when an Amazon Prime parcel, delivered by an Amazon driver (those soulless people in the blue vans), was supposed to have delivered my small parcel between a set time 1pm and 3:30pm.It got down to 1 stop to go by 3pm and the tracker message went blank, which normally happens when you are the next stop, so I watched the van turn up and drive past, thinking it was turning around, so I went to my front door to wave them down, but no sign of them.After about 5 minutes of standing at my doorstep, I went back in and checked the tracker, and the driver was in the middle of the housing estate where I live. So I watched the tracker for the next 30 minutes as the driver circled the estate in his van, driving past my front door twice. The tracker was still showing as blank (where the 'stops' information bubble was). Then, after the 30 minutes were up, he left the estate. This was when it had reached the deadline of the delivery slot at 3:30pm.I then watched the GPS tracker for the next 4 hours, yes 4 hours, during this time the tracker was showing as ‘there are a few more deliveries before we reach you’ display....by then, it was 7:30pm and the van turned up outside my home, I saw it and went to our front door, all this caught on my Blink CCTV camera, the driver didn’t move from his van, he sat there for 2 minutes and by the time I opened our front door he had started to do a U-turn and left. And I went in to check the tracker and the GPS map had gone, and it looked like he was heading back to the depot, with the tracker still showing as ;Still arriving today’. By 10pm, the tracker was shown to ‘Now arriving tomorrow. Previously expected 31 July. We’re sorry for the delay.’ Not good enough Amazon!I’m not sure if this Hipster-looking driver knew what he was doing, but that was extremely poor service. But I have had enough of Amazon and have cancelled my Amazon Prime and requested a refund on the waiting parcel.I will never use Amazon again until they improve their delivery service. Up until then, I have never had any issues with Amazon drivers finding my address.

review_title: Misleading Laptop Listing and Failed Refund – Amazon and Third-Party Seller (Smoothwin Tech)
review_text: I purchased a laptop from Amazon that was sold by a third-party seller, Smoothwin Tech. Upon receiving the item, I immediately turned it on to verify the specifications. I discovered that the processor was a much cheaper model and did not match the specifications advertised on the Amazon product page.I promptly returned the item and reported the issue to Amazon. I received an email from Amazon confirming that the refund had been issued. However, seven days later, after contacting Amazon support to follow up, I was told no refund was ever processed. The representative placed the blame entirely on the third-party seller, Smoothwin Tech.While I understand that the seller was the source of the deception, Amazon is the platform that facilitated the transaction, advertised the item, processed my payment, and confirmed the refund. Amazon failed to supervise this seller and did not uphold its responsibility to protect the customer, especially considering I am a Prime member paying for a premium level of service and trust.This experience has severely undermined my trust in Amazon. I am filing complaint with BBB to report the misleading practices and to hold both Amazon and Smoothwin Tech accountable.

Hi all. I’m an international customer. First time Amazon customer. Initially when I look at the reviews here, I was quite skeptical about placing an order with them. But, decided to take the risk and was pleasantly surprised by the outcome. My own experience has been very satisfying. I am very happy and would strongly recommend others to use/consider Amazon.During the month (July), I’ve ordered 8 books under two different orders. Amazon takes about 48 hours to process my order (the first order) and my second order was immediately processed (same day). The books are shipped by 5 different shipments (different Amazon stores across the States).Three different shipping companies are used. DHL, ECMS, and SF (of the three shipping companies, I prefer best SF, followed by ECMS, and DHL the least). I have to wait for only 7 days to safely receive the shipment. Very efficient. Tracking was provided (via Amazon e-mail – your order confirmation e-mail). You may also check for the tracking on the respective shipping companies’ websites. The progress are very much up-to-date. You’ll always know where your parcel is at all times, until after it reaches your doorstep.All the books are as described. In a perfectly new condition. Prices are reasonable. The only thing that Amazon could improve on is the packaging. Maybe they could add some security features i.e. bubble wrap or something to better protect books during shipment. All-in-all, I am a very happy customer and will definitely be Amazon returning customer.

review_title: Disappointing for a Company Like Amazon
review_text: I’m really disappointed with the Amazon Fresh store on Chancery Lane. While it looks modern and well-presented, the experience falls apart—especially during warmer days. Every time the temperature hits around 26–30°C, the fridges break down, and there’s hardly any fresh food available. Just empty shelves and piles of spoiled food.What’s worse is that this isn’t a one-off—it’s been happening all summer. Meanwhile, other supermarkets like Sainsbury’s, Tesco, M&S, and even Iceland manage to keep their fridges running without a problem. How can a company as big and resourceful as Amazon not get this right?I usually walk home and stop by to grab something fresh to eat, but time and time again, I’m met with disappointment. The only things left are junk food and snacks, and the rest of the store feels like it’s halfway shut down. The level of waste is shocking too—basketloads of spoiled items that clearly couldn’t be sold in time.It’s honestly unacceptable from a multi-billion-pound company. They really need to sort this out.

review_title: It almost seems silly to write yet…
review_text: It almost seems silly to write yet another negative review of Amazon's customer service. But I ran into an unusual issue today. I needed to change the payment method on an order I placed with Amazon. This is nothing new, we often change payment to take advantage of offers or lower interest, etc. Normally this is done on your order page. However for this particular order, there was no option to edit the payment method.Of coarse Amazon does not make it easy to contact someone. I always know I am dealing with a scam site when there is no customer service link. So the fact that Amazon does not have one is very surprising.There are  links to the help pages with answers to essential stupid questions. The answers are obvious in most cases and if you have shopped with Amazon more than once you already know those answers. It is the unusual cases that help is needed. So after a Google search, I found a phone number. I see Amazon has shifted their customer service off shore. After explaining what I needed, the service rep kept asking if I received a notification from my bank. What training does Amazon give these people. The only reason I receive notifications from my bank are suspected fraud, or there is insufficient funds or issues like that. I never hear from them when a transaction goes through.After a useless AI that kept saying I didn't get that, I was ready to throw my phone out the window. Then I get the useless guy.This is why I shop at Walmart's site more often than Amazon. I can get the same stuff same or sometimes better pricing, and Walmart's customer service actually provides service.Bad form Amazon.

review_title: You ever try to sign up for Amazon…
review_text: You ever try to sign up for Amazon Prime with one of their glorious discounted memberships? You know, the ones they advertise for folks using EBT or other assistance programs? I did. Or, at least, I tried. Spoiler alert: I got the full boss-level runaround and no discount in sight.First, I made a brand new account under the name that matched my EBT card—because, as Amazon themselves advised, that's the only way it’d be accepted. Fine. Reasonable. So I set up the account, followed every instruction, and attempted to verify eligibility.And that’s where I entered the customer service maze.Here’s the play-by-play of what followed:Spoke to G. Passed to S.Handed off to C, then back to G.Rebounded to S, then G (AGAIN).Introduced to K, re-routed to C, then K one final time.…then, nothing. Absolute radio silence.At this point I’m pretty sure they either (a) think I’m trying to hack into my own account, or (b) ran out of canned copy-paste responses and short-circuited.When I finally proved—with screenshots, matching emails, and more patience than a monk—that I was the rightful account holder and qualified for the discount, they simply...vanished. Ghosted. Poof. Not even a “thanks for contacting us.” Just silence.I’m a long-time Amazon customer. I’ve used their services for over a decade. I’ve jumped through their hoops, walked their tightropes, and smiled politely while being treated like a suspicious freeloader.All for a discount they themselves said I qualified for.If this is how Amazon handles discount eligibility—by gaslighting and stalling until you give up—then maybe they should stop advertising those discounts like it’s a public service. Because right now? It’s feeling more like a bait-and-switch.
